verification.note: Reported by the WiSig authors themselves (Hanna, Karunaratne, Cabric, 'WiSig: A Large-Scale WiFi Signal Dataset for Receiver and Channel Agnostic RF Fingerprinting', IEEE Access, vol. 10, pp. 22808-22818, 2022, DOI 10.1109/ACCESS.2022.3154790, arXiv:2112.15363), NOT reproduced or re-run by this repo -- this is a from_paper literature row, distinct from our own paper-faithful CNN reproduction already on the board as the self_reported `wisig_cnn_paper` row (same task/track/split; different model.name to avoid any collision). Source: Section IV.D 'Impact of Number of Transmitters' (the ManyTx / `d006` experiment, non-equalized data, all days and all receivers pooled), verified via direct PDF text extraction of arXiv:2112.15363v3. Verbatim: 'When using 4 days, 19 Rx, and only 50 signal per Tx per Rx per day, the classification accuracy with just 10 Tx is around 80%. ... As we increase the number of Tx, the problem becomes more challenging and the accuracy drops to about 53% with 150 Tx.' This corresponds to Fig. 14 ('classification accuracy as a function of the number of transmitters N ... Non-equalized data from all days and receivers in ManyTx is used for both training and evaluation'). DATASET/PROTOCOL MATCH: our canonical split sei-wisig-closedset-strat-tx-8010-seed42-v1 (511515 items total, ~51145 held-out test items per the sibling complex_cnn/wisig_cnn_paper rows) is a stratified-by-Tx split over ManyTx non-equalized, all-rx/all-days pooled data with ~140 transmitter classes -- matching the paper's N=150-Tx ManyTx endpoint (docs/BIBLIOGRAPHY.md A.3 lists `d006` as 150 classes), not the N=10-Tx point, so the ~53% figure (not ~80%) is the one used here as metrics.values.rank1_accuracy. METRIC MATCH: the paper reports classification accuracy on held-out test signals, which is our rank1_accuracy; no unit mismatch. CAVEAT (why this stays an approximate reading, not an exact decimal): the paper states this value in prose as an approximate readout of Fig. 14 ('about 53% with 150 Tx'), not from a separate results table -- no exact tabulated value (e.g. 0.53X) exists anywhere in the text, so 0.53 here is the most precise figure the source itself gives, not a derived or interpolated number. The class-count match (~140 in our split vs N=150 in the paper) is close but not exact; we could not confirm the paper used precisely 150 classes for the point this row represents versus a nearby N on the same Fig. 14 curve. No exact tx-count field is present in the split manifest itself (leaderboard/splits/wisig/sei-wisig-closedset-strat-tx-8010-seed42-v1.manifest.json only lists n_items and checksum), so the ~140-Tx figure is taken from context (existing board rows on this split), not independently re-derived here. Per doctrine, this approximation does not disqualify the from_paper tier (which tolerates unpublished sample-level indices/exact provenance as long as dataset+protocol match); it is disclosed here instead. verified_by / verified_date / verified_hardware are intentionally absent -- no re-run occurred. Model size: n_params=50632 (high confidence — Derived exactly from the OFFICIAL WiSig code d006_ManyTx_ntx.py (github.com/WiSig-dataset/wisig-examples, master branch, function create_net): Input(256,2)->Res…)
